questions.q: Who proposed the two rival 17th–18th century models of light, and what were they?
options: Newton proposed a wave model; Huygens proposed a particle model
Newton proposed a particle (corpuscular) model; Huygens proposed a wave model
Maxwell proposed a particle model; Hertz proposed a wave model
Both Newton and Huygens proposed particle models
questions.explanation: Isaac Newton's corpuscular (particle) model held that light is a stream of tiny particles; Christiaan Huygens proposed that light is a wave. Newton's reputation kept the particle model dominant for nearly a century despite Huygens's theory.

questions.q: Why could reflection and refraction NOT be used to decide between the particle and wave models of light?
options: Because neither model could explain them
Because both rival models could explain reflection and refraction, so they cannot distinguish between the two
Because reflection and refraction had not yet been observed
Because only the wave model could explain them
questions.explanation: Both the particle and wave models successfully accounted for reflection and ordinary refraction. A phenomenon explained by both rivals is useless as evidence for either — the decisive evidence is what only one model can explain.

questions.q: Newton's particle model and Huygens's wave model made opposite predictions about the speed of light in water. What did each predict?
options: Particle model: slower in water; wave model: faster in water
Particle model: faster in water; wave model: slower in water
Both predicted faster in water
Both predicted the same speed as in air
questions.explanation: The particle model required light to speed up in water (the medium imagined to attract the particles); the wave model required it to slow down (the wavefront slowed by the denser medium). These contradictory predictions made refraction into water a decisive test.

questions.q: Whose 1850 measurement resolved the dispute about the speed of light in water, and which model did it support?
options: Newton measured light faster in water, supporting the particle model
Foucault measured light slower in water, supporting the wave model
Young measured light faster in water, supporting the wave model
Hertz measured light slower in water, supporting the particle model
questions.explanation: In 1850 Foucault (and independently Fizeau) measured light to be slower in water than in air, refuting the particle model's prediction and confirming the wave model. The wave model's prediction — slower — turned out to be true.

questions.q: Why can a particle model not explain diffraction?
options: Because particles cannot reflect off surfaces
Because particles travel in straight lines and have no mechanism to bend around a corner into a shadow
Because particles cannot travel through a vacuum
Because particles always cancel one another
questions.explanation: Diffraction is light spreading around edges and through narrow gaps into the shadow region, explained by Huygens's principle. Straight-line particles, once emitted, cannot bend around corners, so they cannot reach into the shadow as the wave does.

questions.q: In Young's double-slit experiment, which feature of the pattern is the decisive evidence against the particle model, and why?
options: The bright bands, because particles cannot add together
The dark bands, because two streams of particles can only add to give more light, never cancel to give darkness
The colour of the fringes, because particles have no colour
The width of the central maximum
questions.explanation: Particles could pile up to make bright spots, so bright bands alone are consistent with particles. But at a dark band, light from both slits combines to give darkness — particles can never subtract or cancel, so the dark bands are the decisive wave evidence.

questions.q: What does it mean for the two slits in Young's experiment to be coherent?
options: They are the same distance from the screen
They emit light of the same wavelength with a constant (fixed) phase relationship
They are very brightly illuminated
They emit light of different colours
questions.explanation: Coherent means monochromatic (a single wavelength) AND a constant phase relationship. Young achieved this by splitting one source into two, so both slits stayed locked in phase. Two independent lamps would be incoherent and wash out the pattern.

questions.q: Why is polarisation considered to give more information than diffraction or interference alone?
options: Because it is easier to demonstrate
Because it not only confirms light is a wave but also proves the wave is transverse, since longitudinal waves cannot be polarised
Because it proves light is a particle
Because it works for sound waves too
questions.explanation: Diffraction and interference show light is a wave but not whether it is transverse or longitudinal. Polarisation does both: a particle stream has nothing to filter, and only a transverse wave has a plane that a filter can select — a longitudinal wave cannot be polarised. This also corrected Huygens, who thought light was longitudinal.

questions.q: The NESA verb 'evaluate' demands more than listing the three pillars. What two things must an evaluation include?
options: A diagram and a calculation
A stated verdict (judgement) and the model's limitation
The names of every physicist and every date
A description of reflection and refraction only
questions.explanation: To evaluate is to make a criteria-based judgement: state a clear verdict on how strongly the wave model is supported, and weigh its successes against its limitation. A list of phenomena, however well explained, is not an evaluation.

questions.q: What is the key limitation of the wave model that a full evaluation must mention?
options: It cannot explain reflection or refraction
It fails to explain black-body radiation and the photoelectric effect, which require light to behave as discrete photons
It cannot explain diffraction
It predicts light travels faster in water
questions.explanation: The wave model explains diffraction, interference and polarisation perfectly, but fails on black-body radiation (the ultraviolet catastrophe) and the photoelectric effect, both of which require the particle-like photon of the quantum model. So the wave model is strongly supported but not a complete description of light.
